---
title: "Managing a large Quarto website: freeze, parallel renders, and the templating trap"
date: 06/15/2026
categories:
  - R
  - Quarto
image: quarto-lessons-thumbnail.png
format:
  html:
    code-fold: show
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

Quarto has slowly become one of my daily tools as a data scientist. Pretty much any analytical task involving data analysis - I turn to quarto instead of jupyter notebook or rmarkdown. More than that, quarto is not just for "notebook" analysis, but can be used to create wiki-like websites with analytical content. Quarto is desigend exactly for this type of product, enabling reproducibility, integration with python, R, and web technologies etc.

Quarto has made my life much easier in a lot of ways, but because I use it so much, I spend a lot of my time fighting with quarto, specifically on limitations around scalability and preformance. This is what this blog post is about and some of the strategies I've been employing to tackle those. 

This is not undocumented, others have discussed the scalability issues (cite blog post, gh discussion), and the quarto team is aware, and seems to planning some significant performance-related upgrades in the near term (crossing fingers that these issues become irrelevant super quick). 

## Quarto for a large >100 page scientific website

I manage a project that involves creating and updating webpages on a quarto website where there are close to 100 pages of analytical documents. "Document" here refers to a `.qmd`[^1] that programmatically (through R, python, and/or javascript) analyses data and generates outputs like graphs and figures that conveniently live alongside narrative - a perfect use-case for quarto. Two types of events prompt site updates:

- Regular data refreshes (weekly, monthly) trigger specific  re-rendering of downstream pages. 
- New scientific questions requires new dedicated webpages for associated analyses to live. 

Each page takes about 20 seconds to 1 minute to render. The total time it takes to render the whole site is **10-20 minutes**, and expected to increase at least 2-fold in the next year. This presents a significant bottleneck in this analytical workflow, where render time requires significant consideration.

[^1]: `.qmd` files are like jupyter notebooks, code and it's output lives together alongside author-written narratives. the "`md`" part conveys that the filetype is "markdown" at heart, a simple lightweight human-readable document. The "`q`" standards for "Quarto", which today conveys a wide set of capabilities from languages supported (R, python, julia, observable js) and output file types. 

## Slowness with quarto render

Speed stems from the fact that quarto renders documents serially and cannot be parallelized due to concurrency problems [Quarto Discussions #2749](https://github.com/orgs/quarto-dev/discussions/2749). A few github issues describe some bespoke solutions[^2], but none are that generalizable. Specifically, they don't work for me, because 1. the solutions are for standalone html documents not a full on website, and 2. I use [`includes`](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/includes.html) to keep my website DRY, which brings some complications to parallelization. 

[^2]: [Quarto CLI #5861](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/issues/5861) and [#4730](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/issues/4730) describe concurrency issues, with some members suggesting workarounds, but with no official fix from quarto yet.

One community workaround (from [@mschubert](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/issues/4730#issuecomment-1469364296)) symlinks the `.qmd` under a unique name, renders that, then cleans up:

```bash
ln -s template.qmd page_A.qmd
quarto render page_A.qmd -P counter:1 --output page_A.html
unlink page_A.qmd
```

Though this works for standalone parameterized reports, it breaks in a Quarto website projects because website outputs lands in a shared `_site/` directory. It also doesn't handle the project-level dependencies those renders need: `_quarto.yml`, `.Rprofile`, `renv/`, `R/` helper functions, and shared data. The following `render_in_isolation` function tries to address these issues to allow parallel rendering for quarto websites.

## `render_in_isolation()`

The idea is: for each `.qmd` you want to render, create a fresh tempdir, symlink in the project resources it needs, render inside that sandbox, and copy the results back.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A["Project directory<br/>(_quarto.yml, R/, data/, …)"]
    B["render_in_isolation('page.qmd')"]
    C["tempdir('/tmp/render-XXXX')"]
    D["Symlink project resources<br/>into tempdir"]
    E["Symlink target .qmd<br/>into tempdir"]
    F["quarto::quarto_render('page.qmd')"]
    G["_freeze/page/ produced<br/>in tempdir"]
    H["Results copied back<br/>to project _freeze/ and _site/"]
    I["tempdir deleted"]
    J["Done"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
```

Here I show pairing with `{targets}` to run in parallel:

```r
library(targets)
source("render_in_isolation.R")

list(
  tar_target(
    pages,
    c("reports/page1.qmd", "reports/page2.qmd", "reports/page3.qmd")
  ),
  tar_target(
    rendered,
    render_in_isolation(pages), # instead of quarto::render
    pattern = map(pages)
  )
)
```

## How it works under the hood

The full source is in [`render_in_isolation.R`](render_in_isolation.R). Here are the key pieces.

### The core function

```r
render_in_isolation <- function(qmd_path, project_dir = ".", 
                                symlinks = NULL, keep_failed = FALSE, ...) {
  project_dir <- fs::path_abs(project_dir)
  tempdir_path <- tempfile("render-", tmpdir = "/tmp")
  dir.create(tempdir_path)
  render_ok <- FALSE
  start <- Sys.time()
  
  on.exit({
    if (!render_ok && keep_failed) return()
    if (fs::dir_exists(tempdir_path)) fs::dir_delete(tempdir_path)
  }, add = TRUE)
  
  # Symlink project resources and template files into the sandbox
  links <- c(DEFAULT_SYMLINKS, 
    list.files(project_dir, pattern = "^_.*\\.(qmd|Rmd)$", full.names = FALSE))
  for (link in links) {
    src <- fs::path(project_dir, link)
    if (fs::file_exists(src) || fs::dir_exists(src)) {
      fs::link_create(fs::path_abs(src), fs::path(tempdir_path, link))
    }
  }
  
  # Render inside the sandbox
  original_wd <- getwd()
  setwd(tempdir_path)
  on.exit(setwd(original_wd), add = TRUE)
  
  result <- tryCatch({
    quarto::quarto_render(fs::path(project_dir, qmd_path), ...)
    
    # Copy freeze and output back to real project
    qmd_no_ext <- sub("\\.qmd$", "", qmd_path)
    freeze_src <- fs::path(tempdir_path, "_freeze", qmd_no_ext)
    freeze_dest <- fs::path(project_dir, "_freeze", qmd_no_ext)
    if (fs::dir_exists(freeze_dest)) fs::dir_delete(freeze_dest)
    fs::dir_create(fs::path_dir(freeze_dest), recurse = TRUE)
    fs::dir_copy(freeze_src, freeze_dest)
    
    # Copy rendered HTML and associated _files/ from _site/
    html_name <- paste0(qmd_no_ext, ".html")
    html_src <- fs::path(tempdir_path, "_site", html_name)
    files_src <- fs::path(tempdir_path, "_site", paste0(qmd_no_ext, "_files"))
    if (fs::file_exists(html_src)) {
      fs::file_copy(html_src, fs::path(project_dir, "_site", html_name), overwrite = TRUE)
    }
    if (fs::dir_exists(files_src)) {
      files_dest <- fs::path(project_dir, "_site", paste0(qmd_no_ext, "_files"))
      if (fs::dir_exists(files_dest)) fs::dir_delete(files_dest)
      fs::dir_copy(files_src, files_dest)
    }
    
    render_ok <<- TRUE
  }, error = function(e) e)
  
  list(
    qmd = qmd_path,
    success = render_ok,
    error = if (render_ok) NULL else result$message,
    duration_sec = as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), start, units = "secs"))
  )
}
```

Three principles drive the design:

### Symlinking project resources

The function populates a tempdir with symbolic links to everything a Quarto render might need:

```r
DEFAULT_SYMLINKS <- c(
  "_quarto.yml",
  "_brand.yml",
  ".Rprofile",
  "R",
  "DESCRIPTION",
  "NAMESPACE",
  "renv",
  "renv.lock",
  "brand",
  "data",
  "includes",
  "img",
  "figures",
  "figure",
  "styles.css",
  "scripts",
  "references.bib"
)

```

::: {.callout-note}
This is a non-exhaustive list of resources that I needed for my own website - add more as needed
:::

It also auto-detects root-level `_*.qmd` and `_*.Rmd` template files, plus any sibling templates in the target's directory. Missing paths are silently skipped.

### Freeze and output management

After `quarto::quarto_render()` completes, the function copies `_freeze/<page>/` and the rendered HTML (from the tempdir's `_site/`) back into the real project. This is what makes `freeze: true` work across parallel renders — each page's freeze data arrives back to the right place without conflicts.

### Error handling

The function returns a list with `$success`, `$error`, and `$duration_sec`. With `keep_failed = TRUE`, the tempdir is preserved for debugging. Crucially, tempdirs are created in `/tmp` (not R's session temp), so they survive the R process exiting.

## Other performance tips

- `freeze` helps a lot, but it only covers the computation part, quarto still spends a lot of time rendering (1-2s per page adds up when you have 100+pages). 
  - BUT, sharing freeze artifacts with git (quarto's suggestion) quickly becomes unwieldy, I recommend sharing via some other mechanism.
- CI/CD with github actions to handle full site renders
- serving the _site directly and running `quarto render [PAGE]` from cli is much much faster than `quarto preview` as this excellent blog post explains. This tip is for increasing speed in the development environment; not relevant for production rendering.  

```bash
# In your project directory
caddy file-server --listen :4210 --root _site
```


## References

- [Quarto — Managing Execution (freeze)](https://quarto.org/docs/projects/code-execution.html#freeze)
- [The targets R package](https://books.ropensci.org/targets/) (Landau, 2021. JOSS 6(57): 2959)
- [Quarto Discussions #2749](https://github.com/orgs/quarto-dev/discussions/2749) — Parallel rendering feature request.
- [Quarto CLI #5861](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/issues/5861) — HPC parallel render bug.
- [Quarto CLI #4730](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/issues/4730) — Parameterized parallel report conflicts.


## scratch


 This issue and related issues has been raised before, and members of the quarto team are working on it, but the changes required are apparently complex. Unfortunately, for large quarto websites like some that I have been maintaining, this limitation represents a rather large bottleneck. Some days it feels like quarto, instead of enabling my data science workflows, feels like it's punishing me for choosing it as part of my stack. But I'm so far entrenched in it, that the migration cost of exploring alternative options is not really that tenable.


 
### Reusing notebooks

Before discussing the problems, one needs to understand how we got here in the first place. Part of the current situation is a result of following quarto's own recommendations on a few points. The first is trying to follow DRY principles and use nifty quarto-specific features like "includes". <!--- cite https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/includes.html --->

In short, `qmd` documents themselves can be treated like functions, they can be parameterized, and be shared as reusable components that can be called from other `qmd` documents. 

So you might have an analysis document called `penguins.qmd` that analyses penguin heights, and then you might have a series of "species" documents that call `penguins.qmd` to analysis height for specific species: 

<!--- insert a file system showing tempalte and child documents + a short snippet of the template / child --->


The "includes" feature is a really powerful way to generalize analysis to new datasets or to be run with different parameters. It enables the implementation of DRY principles in "analysis notebook" workflows. 

**BUT** _The problem with `includes`_ is that you can't parallelize rendering multiple documents that reference the same document. This issue stems rendering a quarto document produces many intermediate files during rendering. Run those renders in parallel and each process overwrites the others' intermediate state mid-render — they clash and error out. 

So *includes* enables reusing code
